edges.description: Loss of AGC2 transport function disrupts hepatic MAS redox transfer and aspartate export.
edges.target: Malate-aspartate shuttle dysfunction and hepatocellular energy crisis

edges.description: Defective carrier exchange directly reduces cytosolic aspartate availability.
edges.target: Aspartate and glutamate transmembrane transport disruption

edges.description: MAS failure impairs cytosolic NADH disposal needed for glycolytic flux.
edges.source: Malate-aspartate shuttle dysfunction and hepatocellular energy crisis
edges.target: Impaired hepatic glycolysis and carbohydrate toxicity

edges.description: Hepatocellular redox and energy failure disrupts lipogenesis and secondarily impairs beta-oxidation.
edges.source: Malate-aspartate shuttle dysfunction and hepatocellular energy crisis
edges.target: De novo lipogenesis defects and PPARalpha-mediated beta-oxidation impairment

edges.description: Reduced aspartate export and hepatocellular ATP deficiency limit urea-cycle nitrogen disposal.
intermediate_mechanisms: Cytosolic aspartate normally supplies one nitrogen atom for ureagenesis.
edges.source: Malate-aspartate shuttle dysfunction and hepatocellular energy crisis
edges.target: Impaired urea cycle and ammonia detoxification

edges.description: Hepatocyte energy and redox failure presents in infancy as transient cholestatic liver disease.
edges.source: Malate-aspartate shuttle dysfunction and hepatocellular energy crisis

edges.description: Hepatocellular energy deficiency contributes to growth impairment and failure to thrive.
intermediate_mechanisms: Inability to efficiently use glucose and free fatty acids limits hepatic energy availability.
edges.source: Malate-aspartate shuttle dysfunction and hepatocellular energy crisis

edges.description: Recurrent metabolic energy failure and hyperammonemic episodes create risk for irreversible brain injury.
intermediate_mechanisms: Energy deficiency and episodic hyperammonemia can injure the developing or adult brain.
edges.source: Malate-aspartate shuttle dysfunction and hepatocellular energy crisis

edges.description: High carbohydrate load worsens ATP depletion, reducing energy available for hepatic ammonia detoxification.
edges.source: Impaired hepatic glycolysis and carbohydrate toxicity
edges.target: Impaired urea cycle and ammonia detoxification

edges.description: Protein- and lipid-rich food preference with carbohydrate aversion reflects physiologic avoidance of carbohydrate toxicity.
edges.source: Impaired hepatic glycolysis and carbohydrate toxicity

edges.description: Impaired glycolysis, gluconeogenesis, and hepatic energy handling contribute to recurrent hypoglycemia.
edges.source: Impaired hepatic glycolysis and carbohydrate toxicity

edges.description: Failed hepatic ureagenesis manifests clinically as recurrent hyperammonemia, especially in CTLN2.
edges.source: Impaired urea cycle and ammonia detoxification

edges.description: Recurrent hyperammonemia in CTLN2 produces neuropsychiatric and neurologic manifestations captured as hyperammonemic encephalopathy.
edges.source: Impaired urea cycle and ammonia detoxification

edges.description: Severe CTLN2 hyperammonemic neurologic episodes can include convulsive seizures.
edges.source: Impaired urea cycle and ammonia detoxification

edges.description: Impaired ureagenesis raises blood or plasma ammonia concentration.
edges.source: Impaired urea cycle and ammonia detoxification

edges.description: Limited cytosolic aspartate availability at ASS1 causes citrulline accumulation.
edges.source: Impaired urea cycle and ammonia detoxification

edges.description: ATP and glutamate limitation can blunt glutamine-synthetase-dependent ammonia detoxification, producing absent or low glutamine elevation despite hyperammonemia.
intermediate_mechanisms: Perivenous glutamine synthetase depends on adequate glutamate and ATP substrates.
edges.source: Impaired urea cycle and ammonia detoxification

edges.description: Pancreatitis is a recognized CTLN2 complication, but the intermediate causal steps are not fully resolved in the cached evidence.
edges.source: Impaired urea cycle and ammonia detoxification

edges.description: Impaired de novo lipogenesis and beta-oxidation disrupt circulating lipid metabolism.
edges.source: De novo lipogenesis defects and PPARalpha-mediated beta-oxidation impairment

edges.description: Defective lipid synthesis and fatty-acid oxidation contribute to diffuse fatty liver and severe liver steatosis.
edges.source: De novo lipogenesis defects and PPARalpha-mediated beta-oxidation impairment

edges.description: Reduced mitochondrial export of aspartate limits aspartate input into the urea cycle.
edges.source: Aspartate and glutamate transmembrane transport disruption
edges.target: Impaired urea cycle and ammonia detoxification

edges.description: Transient intrahepatic cholestasis is the defining neonatal presentation.
Cholestatic liver dysfunction presents clinically with neonatal jaundice.
Infantile cholestatic hepatopathy includes liver enlargement.
Intrahepatic cholestasis causes conjugated bilirubin retention.
Variable liver dysfunction in NICCD includes abnormal liver function tests.
Low birth weight with growth restriction is part of the infantile NICCD presentation.
NICCD includes diffuse fatty liver, while CTLN2 can develop severe steatosis.
Hyperammonemia produces the neuropsychiatric and neurologic CTLN2 syndrome.
intermediate_mechanisms: Ammonia neurotoxicity and downstream cerebral metabolic stress.

edges.description: Severe hyperammonemic neurologic episodes can include convulsive seizures.

edges.target: Malate-aspartate shuttle dysfunction and hepatocellular energy crisis
De novo lipogenesis defects and PPARalpha-mediated beta-oxidation impairment
Impaired urea cycle and ammonia detoxification

edges.description: Elevated citrulline reports impaired ASS1 flux from limited cytosolic aspartate in citrin deficiency.
edges.source: Impaired urea cycle and ammonia detoxification

edges.source: Impaired urea cycle and ammonia detoxification

edges.description: Elevated conjugated bilirubin reports the neonatal cholestatic hepatopathy branch.
Low or inappropriately normal plasma glutamine reports impaired glutamine-synthetase-dependent ammonia detoxification.

edges.description: Elevated transaminases report hepatocellular injury in the neonatal cholestatic hepatopathy stage.

edges.source: Novel nonsense, frameshift, and deletion variants

nodes.description: Patients characteristically prefer protein- and lipid-rich foods and show aversion to carbohydrate-rich foods. This dietary self-selection is now understood as a physiological adaptation to the underlying hepatocellular energy deficiency and carbohydrate intolerance.

evidence.reference_title: Citrin Deficiency.
evidence.snippet:
Often citrin deficiency is 
characterized by strong preference for protein-rich and/or lipid-rich foods and 
aversion to carbohydrate-rich foods.

evidence.reference_title: Citrin Deficiency.
evidence.snippet:
characteristic biochemical analytes (increased blood or plasma 
concentration of ammonia, plasma or serum concentration of citrulline and 
arginine, plasma or serum threonine-to-serine ratio)

readouts.interpretation: Elevated ammonia reports impaired hepatic nitrogen disposal and hyperammonemic CTLN2 episodes.
readouts.target: Impaired urea cycle and ammonia detoxification

nodes.description: Citrin (AGC2) normally transports aspartate from the mitochondrial matrix to the cytosol in exchange for glutamate. Loss of this transport function impairs compartmentalized amino acid metabolism, affecting ureagenesis, gluconeogenesis, protein synthesis, and nucleotide biosynthesis.
nodes.id: Aspartate and glutamate transmembrane transport disruption

evidence.reference_title: Aspartate-glutamate carrier 2 (citrin): a role in glucose and amino acid metabolism in the liver.
evidence.snippet:
Aspartate-glutamate carrier 2 (AGC2, citrin) is a mitochondrial carrier 
expressed in the liver that transports aspartate from mitochondria into the 
cytosol in exchange for glutamate.

nodes.description: Intravenous infusion of glycerol- and fructose-containing osmotic agents is contraindicated and potentially lethal in citrin deficiency. These sugars exacerbate the GAPDH-step metabolic block and worsen hepatocyte ATP depletion under conditions of impaired MAS function.
nodes.id: Avoidance of fructose and glycerol infusions

evidence.reference_title: Citrin Deficiency.
evidence.snippet:
Agents/circumstances to avoid: Low-protein and 
high-carbohydrate diets; glycerol, fructose, and glucose infusions due to risk 
of brain edema; alcohol.

evidence.reference_title: Citrin Deficiency.
evidence.snippet:
characteristic biochemical analytes (increased blood or plasma 
concentration of ammonia, plasma or serum concentration of citrulline and 
arginine, plasma or serum threonine-to-serine ratio)

readouts.interpretation: Elevated citrulline reports impaired ASS1 flux from limited cytosolic aspartate in citrin deficiency.
readouts.target: Impaired urea cycle and ammonia detoxification

evidence.reference_title: Six cases of citrin deficiency in Korea.
evidence.snippet:
They were
associated with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, elevated liver enzymes,
hypoalbuminemia, mild hyperammonemia, elevated citrulline, methionine and
threonine.

readouts.interpretation: Elevated conjugated bilirubin reports the neonatal cholestatic hepatopathy branch.

nodes.description: Citrin-deficient hepatocytes have primary defects in de novo lipogenesis and exhibit secondarily downregulated PPARalpha, leading to impaired fatty acid beta-oxidation. This dual impairment contributes to dyslipidemia and hepatic steatosis.
nodes.id: De novo lipogenesis defects and PPARalpha-mediated beta-oxidation impairment

nodes.description: Dyslipidemia is a characteristic feature of FTTDCD and reflects disrupted de novo lipogenesis and fatty acid oxidation in citrin-deficient hepatocytes.

evidence.reference_title: Citrin Deficiency.
evidence.snippet:
in 
older children as failure to thrive and dyslipidemia caused by citrin deficiency 
(FTTDCD)

evidence.reference_title: Clinical manifestation and long-term outcome of citrin deficiency: Report from a nationwide study in Japan.
evidence.snippet: failure to thrive, and dyslipidemia by citrin deficiency (FTTDCD)

nodes.description: Hyperammonemic encephalopathy with neuropsychiatric symptoms is the defining feature of adult-onset CTLN2. Symptoms include confusion, disorientation, seizures, and altered consciousness.

evidence.reference_title: Citrin Deficiency.
evidence.snippet:
in adults as recurrent hyperammonemia with neuropsychiatric 
symptoms in citrullinemia type II (CTLN2).

nodes.description: Growth impairment is a feature of FTTDCD and can also occur in severe NICCD. In a nationwide Japanese study of 222 patients, growth impairment during adolescence was noted particularly in CTLN2 patients.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical manifestation and long-term outcome of citrin deficiency: Report from a nationwide study in Japan.
evidence.snippet:
Patients with citrin deficiency had an increased risk for low weight and length 
at birth, and CTLN2 patients had an increased risk for growth impairment during 
adolescence.

nodes.description: Fatty liver results from impaired de novo lipogenesis and PPARalpha-mediated downregulation of beta-oxidation. It occurs across multiple disease stages.

evidence.reference_title: Citrin Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Complications include severe liver steatosis and pancreatitis.

nodes.description: Liver enlargement is commonly observed in the neonatal (NICCD) stage, associated with cholestatic hepatitis and steatosis.

evidence.reference_title: Citrin Deficiency.
evidence.snippet:
transient 
intrahepatic cholestasis, hepatomegaly, diffuse fatty liver

nodes.description: Elevated plasma ammonia is a defining feature of CTLN2 and can occur in other stages during catabolic stress. It results from impaired ureagenesis due to cytosolic aspartate and ATP deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Citrin Deficiency.
evidence.snippet:
Citrin deficiency can manifest in newborns or infants 
as neonatal intrahepatic cholestasis caused by citrin deficiency (NICCD), in 
older children as failure to thrive and dyslipidemia caused by citrin deficiency 
(FTTDCD), and in adults as recurrent hyperammonemia with neuropsychiatric 
symptoms in citrullinemia type II (CTLN2).

evidence.reference_title: Clinical manifestation and long-term outcome of citrin deficiency: Report from a nationwide study in Japan.
evidence.snippet:
Citrin deficiency is an autosomal recessive disorder caused by mutations in the 
SLC25A13 gene. The disease can present with age-dependent clinical 
manifestations: neonatal intrahepatic cholestasis by citrin deficiency (NICCD), 
failure to thrive, and dyslipidemia by citrin deficiency (FTTDCD), and 
adult-onset type II citrullinemia (CTLN2).

nodes.description: Hypoketotic hypoglycemia is mechanistically linked to impaired glycogenesis, gluconeogenesis, and ketogenesis in citrin-deficient hepatocytes.

evidence.reference_title: Citrin Deficiency.
evidence.snippet:
Laboratory changes are dyslipidemia, recurrent hypoglycemia, increased 
lactate-to-pyruvate ratio

nodes.description: IVS16ins3kb is a hotspot structural variant in SLC25A13 that requires specific copy number variant calling approaches for detection. It is particularly relevant for newborn screening programs in East Asian populations.

evidence.reference_title: Harnessing Next-Generation Sequencing as a Timely and Accurate Second-Tier Screening Test for Newborn Screening of Inborn Errors of Metabolism.
evidence.snippet:
The 
custom-designed NGS panel can detect sequence variants in the relevant genes and 
also specifically screen for the presence of the hotspot variant IVS16ins3kb

nodes.description: Because glycolysis depends on cytosolic NAD+ regenerated via the MAS, citrin-deficient hepatocytes cannot sustain glycolysis efficiently. Under high carbohydrate load, metabolism is blocked at the glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase (GAPDH) step, leading to accumulation of glucose metabolites, depletion of inorganic phosphate, and further ATP loss. This creates an actively harmful state termed carbohydrate toxicity.
nodes.id: Impaired hepatic glycolysis and carbohydrate toxicity

nodes.description: Citrin deficiency limits cytosolic aspartate availability, which is required by argininosuccinate synthetase 1 (ASS1) for the urea cycle. This results in citrulline accumulation and impaired ureagenesis. Additionally, hyperammonemia may be driven by failure of glutamine-synthetase-dependent perivenous ammonia detoxification due to glutamate and ATP deficiency, rather than solely by ASS1 dysfunction.
nodes.id: Impaired urea cycle and ammonia detoxification

evidence.reference_title: Citrin Deficiency.
evidence.snippet:
Citrin deficiency can manifest in newborns or infants 
as neonatal intrahepatic cholestasis caused by citrin deficiency (NICCD), in 
older children as failure to thrive and dyslipidemia caused by citrin deficiency 
(FTTDCD), and in adults as recurrent hyperammonemia with neuropsychiatric 
symptoms in citrullinemia type II (CTLN2).

evidence.reference_title: Pathogenesis and Management of Citrin Deficiency.
evidence.snippet:
Citrin deficiency (CD) is a hereditary disorder caused by SLC25A13 mutations 
that manifests as neonatal intrahepatic cholestasis caused by CD (NICCD), 
failure to thrive and dyslipidemia caused by CD (FTTDCD), and adult-onset type 2 
citrullinemia (CTLN2).

nodes.description: Irreversible brain damage may occur in patients with citrin deficiency, particularly with delayed diagnosis or inadequate treatment. MCT supplementation with minimal carbohydrate diet aims to prevent this.

evidence.reference_title: Pathogenesis and Management of Citrin Deficiency.
evidence.snippet:
patients with CD often exhibit growth impairment and 
irreversible brain and/or liver damage.

nodes.description: Prolonged neonatal jaundice with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia is a cardinal feature of NICCD.

evidence.reference_title: Case report: Three novel variants on SLC25A13 in four infants with neonatal intrahepatic cholestasis caused by citrin deficiency.
evidence.snippet: We report 4 patients with jaundice and low body weight.

nodes.description: Liver transplantation is reserved for patients with severe or refractory CTLN2, recurrent hyperammonemic episodes unresponsive to medical management, or liver failure. In the Japanese nationwide study of 222 patients, transplantation was performed in only 4 patients (1 NICCD, 3 CTLN2) with good response thereafter.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical manifestation and long-term outcome of citrin deficiency: Report from a nationwide study in Japan.
evidence.snippet:
Liver transplantation has been performed in only 4 patients (1 
NICCD, 3 CTLN2) with a good response thereafter.

evidence.reference_title: Citrin Deficiency.
evidence.snippet:
NICCD is generally not severe, and clinical manifestations are 
often resolved by age one year with appropriate treatment, although liver 
failure may still occur; liver transplantation has been required in rare 
instances.

nodes.description: Patients with citrin deficiency have an increased risk for low weight and length at birth, as demonstrated in a nationwide Japanese cohort.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical manifestation and long-term outcome of citrin deficiency: Report from a nationwide study in Japan.
evidence.snippet:
Patients with citrin deficiency had an increased risk for low weight and length 
at birth

nodes.description: Medium-chain triglyceride (MCT) supplementation combined with a diet containing minimal carbohydrates is the primary therapeutic strategy. MCTs provide energy directly to hepatocytes, increase lipogenesis, and activate the malate-citrate shuttle as a compensatory pathway. Early intervention is recommended promptly after diagnosis to prevent irreversible brain and liver damage.

evidence.reference_title: Pathogenesis and Management of Citrin Deficiency.
evidence.snippet:
Medium-chain 
triglyceride (MCT) supplements are effective for treating CD by providing energy 
to hepatocytes, increasing lipogenesis, and activating the malate-citrate 
shuttle.

evidence.reference_title: Pathogenesis and Management of Citrin Deficiency.
evidence.snippet:
MCT supplementation with a diet containing minimal 
carbohydrates is recommended promptly after the diagnosis.

evidence.reference_title: Medium-chain triglycerides supplement therapy with a low-carbohydrate formula can supply energy and enhance ammonia detoxification in the hepatocytes of patients with adult-onset type II citrullinemia.
evidence.snippet:
All the patients' general condition steadily improved 
and five patients with hyperammonemic encephalopathy recovered from 
unconsciousness in a few days.

nodes.description: Loss of citrin impairs cytosolic NADH transfer into mitochondria and reduces cytosolic aspartate, NAD+, and ATP, creating a fundamental hepatocellular energy and redox deficiency.
nodes.id: Malate-aspartate shuttle dysfunction and hepatocellular energy crisis

evidence.reference_title: Aspartate-glutamate carrier 2 (citrin): a role in glucose and amino acid metabolism in the liver.
evidence.snippet:
Through MAS, AGC2 is necessary to 
maintain intracellular redox balance, mitochondrial respiration, and ATP 
synthesis.

nodes.description: Intrahepatic cholestasis is the hallmark neonatal presentation (NICCD), with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, jaundice, and hepatomegaly. Usually resolves by age one year with dietary management.

evidence.reference_title: Citrin Deficiency.
evidence.snippet:
Citrin deficiency can manifest in newborns or infants 
as neonatal intrahepatic cholestasis caused by citrin deficiency (NICCD), in 
older children as failure to thrive and dyslipidemia caused by citrin deficiency 
(FTTDCD), and in adults as recurrent hyperammonemia with neuropsychiatric 
symptoms in citrullinemia type II (CTLN2).

evidence.reference_title: Case report: Three novel variants on SLC25A13 in four infants with neonatal intrahepatic cholestasis caused by citrin deficiency.
evidence.snippet:
Neonatal intrahepatic cholestasis caused by citrin deficiency 
(NICCD) is a common clinical phenotype of citrin deficiency in infants.

nodes.description: In NICCD, citrin deficiency manifests as transient infantile intrahepatic cholestasis with low birth weight or growth restriction, hepatomegaly, fatty liver, hepatic fibrosis or cellular infiltration, variable liver dysfunction, and hypoglycemia.

evidence.reference_title: Citrin Deficiency.
evidence.snippet:
Children younger than age one year 
have a history of low birth weight with growth restriction and transient 
intrahepatic cholestasis, hepatomegaly, diffuse fatty liver, and parenchymal 
cellular infiltration associated with hepatic fibrosis, variable liver 
dysfunction, hypoproteinemia, decreased coagulation factors, anemia, and/or 
hypoglycemia.

nodes.description: The variant spectrum of SLC25A13 continues to expand. A 2023 case series identified three novel variants including a nonsense mutation in exon 17 (c.1803C>G), a frameshift mutation in exon 11 (c.1141delG), and a whole exon 11 deletion.
nodes.id: Novel nonsense, frameshift, and deletion variants

nodes.description: Pancreatitis is a recognized complication in the disease spectrum of citrin deficiency, particularly associated with CTLN2.

evidence.reference_title: Citrin Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Complications include severe liver steatosis and pancreatitis.

nodes.description: Arginine administration decreases blood ammonia concentration in CTLN2, and sodium pyruvate can increase weight and decrease the frequency of hyperammonemia. These pharmacologic measures support ammonia control alongside dietary and MCT-based management.

evidence.reference_title: Citrin Deficiency.
evidence.snippet:
Sodium pyruvate can increase weight and decrease frequency 
of hyperammonemia; arginine administration decreases blood ammonia 
concentration

evidence.reference_title: Medium-chain triglycerides supplement therapy with a low-carbohydrate formula can supply energy and enhance ammonia detoxification in the hepatocytes of patients with adult-onset type II citrullinemia.
evidence.snippet:
Before the treatment, plasma glutamine levels did 
not increase over the normal range and rather decreased to lower than the normal 
range in some patients.

readouts.interpretation: Low or inappropriately normal plasma glutamine reports impaired glutamine-synthetase-dependent ammonia detoxification.
readouts.target: Impaired urea cycle and ammonia detoxification

Citrin deficiency is an autosomal recessive disorder caused by mutations in the 
SLC25A13 gene.

evidence.snippet: SLC25A13 | HGNC:10983 | citrin deficiency | MONDO:0016602 | AR | Definitive

nodes.description: Biallelic SLC25A13 pathogenic variants reduce mitochondrial aspartate-glutamate carrier transport activity.

evidence.reference_title: Aspartate-glutamate carrier 2 (citrin): a role in glucose and amino acid metabolism in the liver.
evidence.snippet:
Aspartate-glutamate carrier 2 (AGC2, citrin) is a mitochondrial carrier 
expressed in the liver that transports aspartate from mitochondria into the

nodes.description: Seizures may occur in the context of severe hyperammonemic episodes in CTLN2 and during neonatal metabolic instability.

evidence.reference_title: Citrin Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: memory loss, disorientation, drowsiness, convulsive seizures, coma

nodes.description: Acute hyperammonemic episodes in CTLN2 require emergency metabolic stabilization with citrin-specific ammonia-lowering measures such as arginine and MCT-based therapy. Care must avoid glucose-, fructose-, or glycerol-containing solutions and high-carbohydrate management that can worsen the metabolic crisis.

evidence.reference_title: Citrin Deficiency.
evidence.snippet:
arginine administration decreases blood ammonia 
concentration; MCT oil can decrease frequency of hyperammonemia

They were
associated with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, elevated liver enzymes,
hypoalbuminemia, mild hyperammonemia, elevated citrulline, methionine and
threonine.
